name: TWA Flight 800
description: TWA Flight 800 was a Boeing 747-100 that exploded and crashed into the Atlantic Ocean near East Moriches, New York, on July 17, 1996, approximately 12 minutes after takeoff from John F. Kennedy International Airport. All 230 people on board were killed. The official investigation, conducted by the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB), concluded that the probable cause of the accident was an explosion of the center wing fuel tank (CWT), resulting from ignition of flammable fuel/air vapors. The ignition source was most likely a short circuit inside the fuel quantity indication system (FQIS). However, alternative theories persist, primarily focusing on the possibility that a missile strike or bomb caused the crash. Proponents of these theories often cite eyewitness accounts of streaks of light ascending towards the aircraft before the explosion, damage patterns inconsistent with an internal explosion, and alleged government cover-ups. The NTSB thoroughly investigated these claims, concluding that the eyewitness accounts were unreliable and likely misinterpreted the aircraft climbing after takeoff, or debris falling from the plane after the initial explosion. They also demonstrated that the damage patterns were consistent with an internal explosion and that no credible evidence supported the missile or bomb theories. Despite the official findings, the alternative theories continue to circulate, fueled by documentaries, books, and online discussions, often alleging government conspiracy and suppression of evidence. These theories tap into broader anxieties about government transparency and the reliability of official narratives in the face of tragedy.
subtitle: Did a missile or bomb down TWA 800?

claims.claim: A missile strike downed TWA Flight 800.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts of streaks of light ascending towards the aircraft; damage patterns inconsistent with an internal explosion.
claims.criticism: NTSB concluded that eyewitness accounts were unreliable and likely misinterpreted the aircraft or debris; damage patterns consistent with CWT explosion; no physical evidence of a missile strike.

claims.claim: A bomb on board caused the explosion.
claims.evidence: Alleged inconsistencies in the wreckage analysis; claims of recovered bomb fragments.
claims.criticism: NTSB's forensic analysis found no evidence of explosives; alleged bomb fragments were either unrelated or misidentified.

claims.claim: The government is covering up the true cause of the crash.
claims.evidence: Allegations of witness intimidation; withholding of evidence; manipulation of the investigation.
claims.criticism: NTSB's investigation was extensive and transparent; no credible evidence supports claims of a cover-up; alternative explanations exist for perceived inconsistencies.

keyFigures.name: James Sanders
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Downing of TWA Flight 800' advocating missile strike theory.

keyFigures.name: Hank Hughes
keyFigures.role: NTSB investigator and lead investigator for TWA Flight 800.

keyFigures.name: Ray Lahr
keyFigures.role: Former United Airlines pilot and investigator who believes a missile caused the crash.

mythologyConnections: The Phoenix (destruction and rebirth - in this case, only destruction)

timeline.event: TWA Flight 800 explodes and crashes into the Atlantic Ocean.
NTSB releases its final report, concluding the cause was a CWT explosion.
A documentary reignites the missile theory, leading to renewed public interest.
